title: Der gute Handel
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 7. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The seventh tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 7), a comic story built on two sound-jokes: a peasant hears frogs croaking „ak, ak“ as a dispute over his seven thalers and flings his money into the pond for them to “count,” then hears a butcher’s dogs barking „was, was“ as a promise to pay for his meat. Cheated at every turn, he blunders his way to the king’s never-laughing daughter, wins her laugh, and turns a promised beating into a fortune — a lucky fool who keeps landing on his feet. The tale carries a period antisemitic caricature (the Jew, „Mauschel“), translated plainly as it stands, neither amplified nor softened. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as Thaler, Thür, Thore, gieng, fieng, ward, wißt, werth, thun and daß.

sentences.t: Ein Bauer, der hatte seine Kuh auf den Markt getrieben und für sieben Thaler verkauft.
sentences.l: A peasant, he had driven his cow to market and sold it for seven thalers.
sentences.i: A peasant had driven his cow to market and sold it for seven thalers.
sentences.n: The resumptive “der hatte” (‘he had’) after the subject is a colloquial, storytelling construction — the pronoun der picks the subject back up before the verb. You will hear this folksy rhythm all through the tale.

sentences.t: Auf dem Heimweg mußte er an einem Teich vorbei, und da hörte er schon von weitem wie die Frösche riefen „ak, ak, ak, ak.“
sentences.l: On the way home he had to pass a pond, and there he heard already from afar how the frogs were calling “ak, ak, ak, ak.”
sentences.i: On his way home he had to pass a pond, and from far off he could already hear the frogs calling “ak, ak, ak, ak.”
sentences.n: The whole joke turns on sound: the frogs’ “ak, ak” rhymes with acht (‘eight’), and the peasant hears them contradicting his count of seven. Keep the German “ak” in mind against acht as you read.

sentences.t: „Ja,“ sprach er für sich, „die schreien auch ins Haberfeld hinein: sieben sinds, die ich gelöst habe, keine acht.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” he spoke to himself, “those too are shouting into the oat-field: seven it is that I have taken in, not eight.”
sentences.i: “Yes,” he said to himself, “they’re just braying nonsense too: it’s seven I got for her, not eight.”
sentences.n: “ins Haberfeld schreien” (literally ‘to cry into the oat-field’) is an old idiom for talking wild nonsense. “sinds” = sind es (‘it is / they are’), one of many clipped contractions in the peasant’s speech.

sentences.t: Als er zu dem Wasser heran kam, rief er ihnen zu „dummes Vieh, das ihr seid! wißt ihrs nicht besser? sieben Thaler sinds und keine acht.“
sentences.l: When he came up to the water, he called out to them “stupid cattle that you are! do you not know it better? seven thalers it is and not eight.”
sentences.i: When he got to the water, he called out to them, “what stupid cattle you are! don’t you know any better? it’s seven thalers, not eight.”
sentences.n: “wißt ihrs” = wißt ihr es (‘do you know it’); wißt is the 1857 spelling of wisst. “heran kam” is the separable verb herankommen (‘to come up, approach’), the prefix left stranded at the end.

sentences.t: Die Frösche blieben aber bei ihrem „ak, ak, ak, ak.“
sentences.l: The frogs, however, stayed with their “ak, ak, ak, ak.”
sentences.i: But the frogs kept to their “ak, ak, ak, ak.”
sentences.n: “bei etwas bleiben” = to stick to / keep to something.

sentences.t: „Nun, wenn ihrs nicht glauben wollt, ich kanns euch vorzählen,“ holte das Geld aus der Tasche und zählte die sieben Thaler ab, immer vierundzwanzig Groschen auf einen.
sentences.l: “Well, if you won’t believe it, I can count it out to you,” took the money out of his pocket and counted off the seven thalers, always twenty-four groschen to one.
sentences.i: “Well, if you won’t believe it, I can count it out for you,” — and he took the money from his pocket and counted off the seven thalers, twenty-four groschen to each.
sentences.n: The narrator drops the subject before “holte” — a brisk storytelling ellipsis (‘[he] took’). A Thaler was worth 24 Groschen, the everyday small coin.

sentences.t: Die Frösche kehrten sich aber nicht an seine Rechnung und riefen abermals „ak, ak, ak, ak.“
sentences.l: The frogs, however, did not turn themselves at his reckoning and called once more “ak, ak, ak, ak.”
sentences.i: But the frogs paid no heed to his reckoning and called out again “ak, ak, ak, ak.”
sentences.n: “sich kehren an” (‘to heed, mind’) is now archaic; the modern idiom is sich kümmern um. “abermals” = ‘once again.’

sentences.t: „Ei,“ rief der Bauer ganz bös, „wollt ihrs besser wissen als ich, so zählt selber,“ und warf ihnen das Geld miteinander ins Wasser hinein.
sentences.l: “Ei,” cried the peasant quite angry, “if you will know it better than I, then count for yourselves,” and threw them the money all together into the water.
sentences.i: “Well then!” cried the peasant, quite cross, “if you know better than I do, count it yourselves,” — and he flung them the whole of the money into the water.
sentences.n: “Ei” is an exclamation of impatience (‘why!’, ‘well!’). “miteinander” here means the coins all together, in one lot.

sentences.t: Er blieb stehen und wollte warten bis sie fertig wären und ihm das Seinige wieder brächten, aber die Frösche beharrten auf ihrem Sinn, schrien immerfort „ak, ak, ak, ak“, und warfen auch das Geld nicht wieder heraus.
sentences.l: He stayed standing and wanted to wait until they were finished and brought him his own back, but the frogs persisted in their notion, screamed continually “ak, ak, ak, ak,” and did not throw the money out again either.
sentences.i: He stood waiting for them to finish and bring him back what was his, but the frogs stuck to their idea, screamed on and on “ak, ak, ak, ak,” and never threw the money back out.
sentences.n: “wären” and “brächten” are subjunctive II (his hoped-for outcome). “das Seinige” = ‘his own, his property’; the possessive is nominalized with the article, a pattern you will meet again (das Meinige, das Ihrige).

sentences.t: Er wartete noch eine gute Weile, bis der Abend anbrach, und er nach Haus mußte, da schimpfte er die Frösche aus und rief „ihr Wasserpatscher, ihr Dickköpfe, ihr Klotzaugen, ein groß Maul habt ihr und könnt schreien daß einem die Ohren weh thun, aber sieben Thaler könnt ihr nicht zählen: meint ihr, ich wollte da stehen bis ihr fertig wärt?“
sentences.l: He waited a good while yet, until evening broke on and he had to go home; then he scolded the frogs and cried “you water-splashers, you thick-heads, you block-eyes, a big mouth you have and can scream so that one’s ears hurt, but seven thalers you cannot count: do you think I would stand here until you were finished?”
sentences.i: He waited a good while longer, till evening came on and he had to go home; then he cursed the frogs and cried, “you water-splashers, you blockheads, you goggle-eyes, a big mouth you’ve got, and you can screech till a body’s ears ache, but seven thalers you can’t count — do you think I’d stand here till you were done?”
sentences.n: “weh thun” (1857 spelling of wehtun, ‘to hurt’) and “einem” (dat. of ‘one’) give ‘so that one’s ears hurt.’ “wärt” is subjunctive II of sein, 2nd person plural (‘you were’).

sentences.t: Damit gieng er fort, aber die Frösche riefen noch „ak, ak, ak, ak“ hinter ihm her, daß er ganz verdrießlich heim kam.
sentences.l: With that he went off, but the frogs still called “ak, ak, ak, ak” after him, so that he came home quite vexed.
sentences.i: With that he went off, but the frogs kept calling “ak, ak, ak, ak” after him, so that he came home thoroughly out of sorts.
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging (‘went’) — you will meet gieng, fieng, anfieng throughout. “hinter ihm her” = ‘after him, at his back.’

sentences.t: Über eine Zeit erhandelte er sich wieder eine Kuh, die schlachtete er, und machte die Rechnung, wenn er das Fleisch gut verkaufte, könnte er so viel lösen, als die beiden Kühe werth wären, und das Fell hätte er obendrein.
sentences.l: After a time he bargained himself another cow; that one he slaughtered, and made the reckoning that, if he sold the meat well, he could take in as much as the two cows were worth, and the hide he would have on top of it.
sentences.i: After a while he traded for another cow; this one he slaughtered, and reckoned that, if he sold the meat well, he could make as much as both cows were worth, and have the hide into the bargain.
sentences.n: “werth” is the 1857 spelling of wert (‘worth’). The clause slides from the peasant’s calculation into subjunctive II (könnte, wären, hätte) — reported reckoning, ‘he figured he could…’

sentences.t: Als er nun mit dem Fleisch zu der Stadt kam, war vor dem Thore ein ganzes Rudel Hunde zusammengelaufen, voran ein großer Windhund: der sprang um das Fleisch, schnupperte und bellte „was, was, was, was.“
sentences.l: When he now came with the meat to the town, there had run together before the gate a whole pack of dogs, at the front a big greyhound: that one sprang about the meat, sniffed and barked “was, was, was, was.”
sentences.i: When he came to town with the meat, a whole pack of dogs had gathered before the gate, a big greyhound in the lead; it leapt about the meat, sniffed, and barked “was, was, was, was.”
sentences.n: A second sound-joke: the dog’s “was, was” is the German was (‘what’), which the peasant will hear as the dogs demanding ‘what?’ / a share. “Thore” is the 1857 spelling of Tore (‘gate’).

sentences.t: Als er gar nicht aufhören wollte, sprach der Bauer zu ihm „ja, ich merke wohl, du sagst „was, was,“ weil du etwas von dem Fleisch verlangst, da sollt ich aber schön ankommen, wenn ich dirs geben wollte.“
sentences.l: When it would not stop at all, the peasant spoke to it “yes, I well notice, you say ‘was, was,’ because you want something of the meat; but there I should fare finely, if I were willing to give it to you.”
sentences.i: When it wouldn’t leave off at all, the peasant said to it, “yes, I can tell — you’re saying ‘what, what,’ because you want some of the meat; but I’d be in a fine way if I gave it to you.”
sentences.n: “da sollt ich schön ankommen” is ironic: schön (‘finely’) means the opposite — ‘I’d come off badly.’ “dirs” = dir es (‘it to you’).

sentences.t: Der Hund antwortete nichts als „was, was.“
sentences.l: The dog answered nothing but “was, was.”
sentences.i: The dog answered nothing but “was, was.”

sentences.t: „Willst dus auch nicht wegfressen und für deine Kameraden da gut stehen?“
sentences.l: “Will you also not gobble it up, and stand good for your comrades there?”
sentences.i: “And you won’t devour it, and you’ll vouch for your comrades here?”
sentences.n: “dus” = du es (‘you it’). “für jemand gut stehen” = to stand surety, be answerable for someone. “wegfressen” uses fressen (‘to eat’ of animals), not essen.

sentences.t: „Was, was“ sprach der Hund.
sentences.l: “Was, was,” spoke the dog.
sentences.i: “Was, was,” said the dog.

sentences.t: „Nun, wenn du dabei beharrst, so will ich dirs lassen, ich kenne dich wohl und weiß bei wem du dienst: aber das sage ich dir, in drei Tagen muß ich mein Geld haben, sonst geht dirs schlimm: du kannst mirs nur hinausbringen.“
sentences.l: “Well, if you persist in it, then I will leave it to you; I know you well and know with whom you serve: but this I tell you, in three days I must have my money, else it goes ill for you: you can just carry it out to me.”
sentences.i: “Well, if you insist on it, I’ll leave it to you; I know you well and know whose dog you are — but I tell you this: in three days I must have my money, or it will go badly for you; you can just bring it out to me.”
sentences.n: The peasant pretends the greyhound belongs to a master he knows (the butcher). “dirs / mirs” = dir es / mir es. “hinausbringen” = to bring/carry out.

sentences.t: Darauf lud er das Fleisch ab und kehrte wieder um: die Hunde machten sich darüber her und bellten laut „was, was.“
sentences.l: Thereupon he unloaded the meat and turned back again: the dogs fell upon it and barked loudly “was, was.”
sentences.i: Then he unloaded the meat and turned back again; the dogs fell upon it and barked loudly “was, was.”
sentences.n: Two separable verbs: “lud … ab” (abladen, ‘to unload’; lud is the past of laden) and “kehrte … um” (umkehren, ‘to turn back’). “sich über etwas hermachen” = to set upon, fall to.

sentences.t: Der Bauer, der es von weitem hörte, sprach zu sich „horch, jetzt verlangen sie alle was, aber der große muß mir einstehen.“
sentences.l: The peasant, who heard it from afar, spoke to himself “hark, now they all want something, but the big one must answer to me.”
sentences.i: The peasant, hearing it from afar, said to himself, “hark — now they all want something; but the big one must answer for it.”
sentences.n: The pun lands here: “verlangen sie alle was” means both ‘they all bark was’ and ‘they all want something.’ “einstehen (für)” = to vouch, be answerable.

sentences.t: Als drei Tage herum waren, dachte der Bauer „heute Abend hast du dein Geld in der Tasche“ und war ganz vergnügt.
sentences.l: When three days were round, the peasant thought “this evening you have your money in your pocket” and was quite content.
sentences.i: When three days were up, the peasant thought, “this evening you’ll have your money in your pocket,” and was well pleased.
sentences.n: “herum sein” (of time) = to be up, over. The peasant addresses himself as “du,” a folksy inner monologue.

sentences.t: Aber es wollte niemand kommen und auszahlen.
sentences.l: But no one would come and pay out.
sentences.i: But no one would come and pay up.
sentences.n: Impersonal “es … niemand”: the es is a placeholder filling the front slot; the real subject is niemand (‘no one’).

sentences.t: „Es ist kein Verlaß mehr auf jemand,“ sprach er, und endlich riß ihm die Geduld, daß er in die Stadt zu dem Fleischer gieng und sein Geld forderte.
sentences.l: “There is no more reliance upon anyone,” he spoke, and at last his patience tore, so that he went into the town to the butcher and demanded his money.
sentences.i: “There’s no relying on anybody anymore,” he said, and at last his patience snapped, so that he went into town to the butcher and demanded his money.
sentences.n: “die Geduld riß ihm” — literally ‘the patience tore for him’; the dative ihm marks whose patience it is (dative of possession), a construction you meet constantly.

sentences.t: Der Fleischer meinte, es wäre ein Spaß, aber der Bauer sagte „Spaß beiseite, ich will mein Geld: hat der große Hund euch nicht die ganze geschlachtete Kuh vor drei Tagen heim gebracht?“
sentences.l: The butcher thought it was a joke, but the peasant said “joke aside, I want my money: did the big dog not bring you home the whole slaughtered cow three days ago?”
sentences.i: The butcher supposed it was a joke, but the peasant said, “joking aside, I want my money — didn’t the big dog bring you the whole slaughtered cow home three days ago?”
sentences.n: “wäre” is subjunctive II of reported thought (‘that it was a joke’). “Spaß beiseite” = ‘joking aside,’ a fixed phrase.

sentences.t: Da ward der Fleischer zornig, griff nach einem Besenstiel und jagte ihn hinaus.
sentences.l: Then the butcher became angry, reached for a broomstick and chased him out.
sentences.i: At that the butcher grew angry, snatched up a broomstick, and drove him out.
sentences.n: “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (‘became’); modern German uses wurde. “jagte … hinaus” = hinausjagen (‘to drive out’), separable.

sentences.t: „Wart,“ sprach der Bauer, „es gibt noch Gerechtigkeit auf der Welt!“ und gieng in das königliche Schloß und bat sich Gehör aus.
sentences.l: “Wait,” spoke the peasant, “there is still justice in the world!” and went into the royal castle and begged himself a hearing.
sentences.i: “Just wait,” said the peasant, “there’s still justice in the world!” — and he went to the royal palace and asked for a hearing.
sentences.n: “Wart” is the clipped imperative of warten (‘wait!’). “sich Gehör ausbitten” = to request a hearing; ausbitten is separable (bat … aus).

sentences.t: Er ward vor den König geführt, der da saß mit seiner Tochter und fragte was ihm für ein Leid wiederfahren wäre?
sentences.l: He was led before the king, who sat there with his daughter and asked what sort of harm had befallen him.
sentences.i: He was brought before the king, who sat there with his daughter and asked what wrong had befallen him.
sentences.n: “was für ein Leid” = ‘what kind of harm’ (the für here does not mean ‘for’). “wiederfahren” is the 1857 spelling of widerfahren (‘to befall’); “wäre” is reported-speech subjunctive.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ sagte er, „die Frösche und die Hunde haben mir das Meinige genommen, und der Metzger hat mich dafür mit dem Stock bezahlt,“ und erzählte weitläufig wie es zugegangen war.
sentences.l: “Ah,” he said, “the frogs and the dogs have taken from me what is mine, and the butcher has paid me for it with the stick,” and told at length how it had come about.
sentences.i: “Ah,” he said, “the frogs and the dogs have taken what was mine, and the butcher paid me for it with a stick,” and he told at length how it had all come to pass.
sentences.n: “mit dem Stock bezahlt” is grim humor — ‘paid with a beating.’ Metzger and Fleischer are both used for ‘butcher.’ “wie es zugegangen war” = ‘how it had happened’ (zugehen, ‘to come about’).

sentences.t: Darüber fieng die Königstochter laut an zu lachen, und der König sprach zu ihm „Recht kann ich dir hier nicht geben, aber dafür sollst du meine Tochter zur Frau haben: ihr Lebtag hat sie noch nicht gelacht, als eben über dich, und ich habe sie dem versprochen, der sie zum Lachen brächte.
sentences.l: At this the king’s daughter began to laugh loudly, and the king spoke to him “I cannot give you justice here, but in return you shall have my daughter for a wife: in all her days she has not yet laughed, except just now at you, and I have promised her to him who should bring her to laughing.
sentences.i: At this the king’s daughter burst out laughing, and the king said to him, “I cannot give you justice here, but in return you shall have my daughter to wife: never in her life has she laughed until now, at you — and I have promised her to whoever should make her laugh.
sentences.n: “fieng … an zu lachen” = anfangen (‘to begin’), 1857 fieng. “ihr Lebtag” = ‘in all her life.’ “brächte” is subjunctive II (‘whoever should bring her to laugh’).

sentences.t: Du kannst Gott für dein Glück danken.“
sentences.l: You can thank God for your luck.”
sentences.i: You may thank God for your good fortune.”
sentences.n: “danken” takes the dative (Gott danken, ‘to thank God’) and für for the thing thanked for.

sentences.t: „O,“ antwortete der Bauer, „ich will sie gar nicht: ich habe daheim nur eine einzige Frau, und die ist mir schon zuviel: wenn ich nach Haus komme, so ist mir nicht anders als ob in jedem Winkel eine stände.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” answered the peasant, “I do not want her at all: I have at home only one single wife, and she is already too much for me: when I come home, it is to me no otherwise than as if in every corner one were standing.”
sentences.i: “Oh,” answered the peasant, “I don’t want her at all: at home I have but a single wife, and she is already too much for me — when I come home, it feels to me as though a wife were standing in every corner.”
sentences.n: “die ist mir schon zuviel” — the dative mir gives ‘too much for me.’ “als ob … stände” is subjunctive II after als ob (‘as if’); stände (‘were standing’) is the subjunctive of stehen.

sentences.t: Da ward der König zornig und sagte „du bist ein Grobian.“
sentences.l: Then the king became angry and said “you are a boor.”
sentences.i: At that the king grew angry and said, “you are a boor.”
sentences.n: “Grobian” (‘rude fellow, lout’) is a mock-Latin coinage from grob (‘coarse’).

sentences.t: „Ach, Herr König,“ antwortete der Bauer, „was könnt Ihr von einem Ochsen anders erwarten, als Rindfleisch!“
sentences.l: “Ah, Lord King,” answered the peasant, “what can you expect otherwise from an ox than beef!”
sentences.i: “Ah, my lord king,” answered the peasant, “what else can you expect from an ox but beef!”
sentences.n: “Ihr” (capitalized) is the old polite form of address to the king (‘you’). The peasant’s retort: a coarse ox naturally yields coarse ‘beef’ — i.e. coarse words.

sentences.t: „Warte,“ erwiederte der König, „du sollst einen andern Lohn haben.
sentences.l: “Wait,” replied the king, “you shall have another reward.
sentences.i: “Just wait,” replied the king, “you shall have a different reward.
sentences.n: “erwiederte” is the 1857 spelling of erwiderte (‘replied’). “Lohn” here means ‘reward, wages’ — with a threatening edge.

sentences.t: Jetzt pack dich fort, aber in drei Tagen komm wieder, so sollen dir fünfhundert vollgezählt werden.“
sentences.l: Now pack yourself off, but in three days come again, then five hundred shall be counted out to you in full.”
sentences.i: Now be off with you, but come again in three days, and five hundred shall be counted out to you in full.”
sentences.n: “sich fortpacken” = to clear off, be gone. The peasant will assume ‘five hundred’ means coins; the king means blows — the trap of the tale.

sentences.t: Wie der Bauer hinaus vor die Thür kam, sprach die Schildwache „du hast die Königstochter zum Lachen gebracht, da wirst du was rechtes bekommen haben.“
sentences.l: As the peasant came out before the door, the sentry spoke “you have brought the king’s daughter to laughing, so you will have got something fine.”
sentences.i: As the peasant came out through the door, the sentry said, “you’ve made the king’s daughter laugh — you must have got yourself something fine.”
sentences.n: “wirst … bekommen haben” is future perfect used to guess about the past (‘you must have got’). “was rechtes” = etwas Rechtes (‘something proper/fine’). “Thür” is the 1857 spelling of Tür.

sentences.t: „Ja, das mein ich,“ antwortete der Bauer, „fünfhundert werden mir ausgezahlt.“
sentences.l: “Yes, that I think,” answered the peasant, “five hundred are being paid out to me.”
sentences.i: “Yes, I should think so,” answered the peasant, “five hundred are to be paid out to me.”
sentences.n: “das mein ich” = ‘that’s what I think / I should say so.’ mein is the clipped meine (‘I mean/think’).

sentences.t: „Hör,“ sprach der Soldat, „gib mir etwas davon: was willst du mit all dem Geld anfangen!“
sentences.l: “Listen,” spoke the soldier, “give me something of it: what will you do with all that money!”
sentences.i: “Listen,” said the soldier, “give me some of it — what will you do with all that money!”
sentences.n: “mit etwas anfangen” = to do with / make of something. Here the sentry becomes ‘the soldier’ — same man.

sentences.t: „Weil dus bist,“ sprach der Bauer, „so sollst du zweihundert haben, melde dich in drei Tagen beim König, und laß dirs aufzählen.“
sentences.l: “Because it is you,” spoke the peasant, “you shall have two hundred; report yourself in three days to the king, and let it be counted out to you.”
sentences.i: “Since it’s you,” said the peasant, “you shall have two hundred; present yourself to the king in three days and have it counted out to you.”
sentences.n: “Weil dus bist” (= du es bist) is a warm colloquialism, ‘seeing as it’s you.’ “sich melden” = to report, present oneself. “laß dirs aufzählen” = ‘have it counted out to you’ — the peasant is unknowingly handing over a share of the beating.

sentences.t: Ein Jude, der in der Nähe gestanden und das Gespräch mit angehört hatte, lief dem Bauer nach, hielt ihn beim Rock und sprach „Gotteswunder, was seid ihr ein Glückskind! ich wills euch wechseln, ich wills euch umsetzen in Scheidemünz, was wollt ihr mit den harten Thalern?“
sentences.l: A Jew, who had stood nearby and had listened in on the conversation, ran after the peasant, held him by the coat and spoke “God’s wonder, what a child of fortune you are! I will change it for you, I will convert it for you into small coin — what do you want with the hard thalers?”
sentences.i: A Jew who had been standing nearby and had listened in on the talk ran after the peasant, caught him by the coat, and said, “God’s wonder, what a lucky fellow you are! I’ll change it for you, I’ll turn it into small coin for you — what do you want with hard thalers?”
sentences.n: This Jewish figure is a period antisemitic caricature typical of 19th-century tales; it is translated plainly here as it stands. “was seid ihr ein Glückskind” = ‘what a lucky child you are.’ “wills” = will es; “Scheidemünz(e)” = small change.

sentences.t: „Mauschel,“ sagte der Bauer, „dreihundert kannst du noch haben, gib mirs gleich in Münze, heut über drei Tage wirst du dafür beim König bezahlt werden.“
sentences.l: “Mauschel,” said the peasant, “three hundred you can have as well; give me it at once in coin — three days from today you will be paid for it at the king’s.”
sentences.i: “Mauschel,” said the peasant, “you can have three hundred more; give it to me at once in coin — three days from now you’ll be paid for it at the king’s.”
sentences.n: “Mauschel” is a derogatory period nickname for a Jew (a slur of the era), rendered as it stands in the source. “heut über drei Tage” = ‘three days from today.’

sentences.t: Der Jude freute sich über das Profitchen und brachte die Summe in schlechten Groschen, wo drei so viel werth sind als zwei gute.
sentences.l: The Jew rejoiced over the little profit and brought the sum in bad groschen, where three are worth as much as two good ones.
sentences.i: The Jew was pleased at his little profit and brought the sum in bad groschen, three of which were worth only as much as two good ones.
sentences.n: “sich freuen über” = to be glad about. “werth” is the 1857 spelling of wert.

sentences.t: Nach Verlauf der drei Tage gieng der Bauer, dem Befehl des Königs gemäß, vor den König.
sentences.l: After the lapse of the three days the peasant went, according to the king’s command, before the king.
sentences.i: When the three days were past, the peasant went before the king, according to the king’s command.
sentences.n: “gemäß” (‘according to’) governs the dative and often follows its noun: “dem Befehl … gemäß.”

sentences.t: „Zieht ihm den Rock aus,“ sprach dieser, „er soll seine fünfhundert haben.“
sentences.l: “Pull off his coat,” spoke this one, “he shall have his five hundred.”
sentences.i: “Take off his coat,” said the king, “he shall have his five hundred.”
sentences.n: “zieht … aus” = ausziehen (‘to take off’), imperative plural to the guards. “dieser” (‘this one’) points back to the king. The stripping is for the blows to come.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ sagte der Bauer, „sie gehören nicht mehr mein, zweihundert habe ich an die Schildwache verschenkt, und dreihundert hat mir der Jude eingewechselt, von Rechtswegen gebührt mir gar nichts.“
sentences.l: “Ah,” said the peasant, “they no longer belong to me: two hundred I have given away to the sentry, and three hundred the Jew has exchanged from me; by rights nothing at all is due to me.”
sentences.i: “Ah,” said the peasant, “they’re no longer mine: two hundred I gave away to the sentry, and three hundred the Jew changed off me — by rights nothing at all is owing to me.”
sentences.n: “gehören … mein” — colloquial for ‘belong to me’ (standard would be mir). “von Rechtswegen” = ‘by rights’; “gebühren” (+ dat.) = to be due to someone.

sentences.t: Indem kam der Soldat und der Jude herein, verlangten das Ihrige, das sie dem Bauer abgewonnen hätten, und erhielten die Schläge richtig zugemessen.
sentences.l: In that moment the soldier and the Jew came in, demanded what was theirs, which they had won from the peasant, and received the blows duly meted out.
sentences.i: Just then the soldier and the Jew came in, demanded what was theirs, which they had won off the peasant, and got the blows duly measured out to them.
sentences.n: “das Ihrige” = ‘their own’ (see das Seinige, das Meinige earlier). “hätten … abgewonnen” is reported-speech subjunctive (‘which they had, so they said, won’). The joke pays off: their ‘shares’ are shares of the beating.

sentences.t: Der Soldat ertrugs geduldig und wußte schon wies schmeckte: der Jude aber that jämmerlich, „au weih geschrien! sind das die harten Thaler?“
sentences.l: The soldier bore it patiently and knew already how it tasted: the Jew, however, carried on piteously, “oh woe cried out! are these the hard thalers?”
sentences.i: The soldier bore it patiently and knew well enough how it tasted; but the Jew set up a piteous howl, “oh, woe is me! are these the hard thalers?”
sentences.n: “ertrugs” = ertrug es; “wies” = wie es; “that” is the 1857 spelling of tat (‘did’; jämmerlich thun = to carry on piteously). “au weih geschrien” is a Yiddish-flavored cry of pain.

sentences.t: Der König mußte über den Bauer lachen, und da aller Zorn verschwunden war, sprach er, „weil du deinen Lohn schon verloren hast, bevor er dir zu Theil ward, so will ich dir einen Ersatz geben: geh in meine Schatzkammer und hol dir Geld, so viel du willst.“
sentences.l: The king had to laugh at the peasant, and since all anger had vanished, he spoke, “because you have already lost your reward before it fell to your share, I will give you a replacement: go into my treasure-chamber and fetch yourself money, as much as you want.”
sentences.i: The king could not help laughing at the peasant, and now that all his anger had vanished, he said, “since you have lost your reward before it ever came to you, I will give you a compensation: go into my treasury and fetch yourself money, as much as you want.”
sentences.n: “jemandem zu Theil werden” (1857 Theil for Teil) = to fall to someone’s share, come to someone. “ward” again = ‘became/was.’ “Ersatz” = replacement, compensation.

sentences.t: Der Bauer ließ sich das nicht zweimal sagen, und füllte in seine weiten Taschen was nur hinein wollte.
sentences.l: The peasant did not let himself be told that twice, and filled into his wide pockets whatever only would go in.
sentences.i: The peasant did not need telling twice, and filled his wide pockets with whatever would go in.
sentences.n: “sich etwas nicht zweimal sagen lassen” = ‘not to need telling twice.’ “was nur hinein wollte” = ‘whatever would go in’ (wollen here of a thing, ‘would’).

sentences.t: Danach gieng er ins Wirthshaus und überzählte sein Geld.
sentences.l: After that he went into the inn and counted over his money.
sentences.i: After that he went to the inn and counted through his money.
sentences.n: “Wirthshaus” is the 1857 spelling of Wirtshaus (‘inn, tavern’). “überzählen” = to count over, count through.

sentences.t: Der Jude war ihm nachgeschlichen und hörte wie er mit sich allein brummte „nun hat mich der Spitzbube von König doch hinters Licht geführt! hätte er mir nicht selbst das Geld geben können, so wüßte ich was ich hätte, wie kann ich nun wissen ob das richtig ist was ich so auf gut Glück eingesteckt habe!“
sentences.l: The Jew had crept after him and heard how he grumbled to himself alone “now the rogue of a king has led me behind the light after all! could he not have given me the money himself, then I would know what I had; how can I now know whether that is right which I have pocketed so on good luck!”
sentences.i: The Jew had crept after him and heard him grumbling to himself, “now the rogue of a king has cheated me after all! could he not have given me the money himself? then I’d know what I had — how can I know now whether what I’ve pocketed at random is right!”
sentences.n: “der Spitzbube von König” = ‘the rogue of a king,’ von linking an epithet to a noun. “hinters Licht führen” (= hinter das Licht) is the idiom ‘to deceive, dupe.’ “auf gut Glück” = ‘at random, on the off-chance.’

sentences.t: „Gott bewahre,“ sprach der Jude für sich, „der spricht despectirlich von unserm Herrn, ich lauf und gebs an, da krieg ich eine Belohnung, und er wird obendrein noch bestraft.“
sentences.l: “God forbid,” spoke the Jew to himself, “he speaks disrespectfully of our lord; I run and report it, then I get a reward, and he is punished into the bargain besides.”
sentences.i: “God forbid,” said the Jew to himself, “he’s speaking disrespectfully of our lord — I’ll run and report it, and I’ll get a reward, and he’ll be punished into the bargain.”
sentences.n: “Gott bewahre” = ‘God forbid.’ “despectirlich” is the archaic spelling of despektierlich (‘disrespectfully’). “gebs an” = gebe es an (angeben, ‘to report, denounce’).

sentences.t: Als der König von den Reden des Bauern hörte, gerieth er in Zorn und hieß den Juden hingehen und den Sünder herbeiholen.
sentences.l: When the king heard of the peasant’s speeches, he fell into anger and bade the Jew go and fetch the sinner here.
sentences.i: When the king heard of the peasant’s words, he flew into a rage and bade the Jew go and fetch the offender.
sentences.n: “gerieth” (1857 for geriet) — “in Zorn geraten” = to fly into a rage. “heißen” here = to bid, command (someone to do something). “Sünder” = ‘sinner, offender.’

sentences.t: Der Jude lief zum Bauer, „ihr sollt gleich zum Herrn König kommen, wie ihr geht und steht.“
sentences.l: The Jew ran to the peasant, “you shall come at once to the lord king, just as you go and stand.”
sentences.i: The Jew ran to the peasant, “you must come at once to the lord king, just as you are.”
sentences.n: “wie ihr geht und steht” = ‘just as you are, then and there.’ “ihr” is the polite/plural ‘you.’

sentences.t: „Ich weiß besser, was sich schickt,“ antwortete der Bauer, „erst laß ich mir einen neuen Rock machen; meinst du ein Mann, der so viel Geld in der Tasche hat, sollte in dem alten Lumpenrock hingehen?“
sentences.l: “I know better what is fitting,” answered the peasant, “first I have a new coat made for myself; do you think a man who has so much money in his pocket should go there in the old ragged coat?”
sentences.i: “I know better what’s proper,” answered the peasant, “first I’ll have a new coat made; do you think a man with so much money in his pocket should go there in his old ragged coat?”
sentences.n: “was sich schickt” = ‘what is fitting, proper.’ “laß ich mir … machen” — the causative lassen, ‘to have (something) made.’ “Lumpenrock” = ragged coat.

sentences.t: Der Jude, als er sah daß der Bauer ohne einen andern Rock nicht wegzubringen war, und weil er fürchtete wenn der Zorn des Königs verraucht wäre, so käme er um seine Belohnung und der Bauer um seine Strafe, so sprach er „ich will euch für die kurze Zeit einen schönen Rock leihen aus bloßer Freundschaft; was thut der Mensch nicht alles aus Liebe!“
sentences.l: The Jew, when he saw that the peasant was not to be got away without another coat, and because he feared that if the king’s anger had blown over, then he would come out of his reward and the peasant out of his punishment, so he spoke “I will lend you a fine coat for the short while out of mere friendship; what does a man not do out of love!”
sentences.i: The Jew, seeing that the peasant would not budge without another coat, and fearing that if the king’s anger had cooled he would lose his reward and the peasant his punishment, said, “I’ll lend you a fine coat for the short while out of pure friendship — what will a man not do out of love!”
sentences.n: “nicht wegzubringen war” = ‘was not to be got away, would not budge.’ “verrauchen” (of anger) = to blow over, cool. “um etwas kommen” = to lose, be done out of something. “thut” = tut (‘does’).

sentences.t: Der Bauer ließ sich das gefallen, zog den Rock vom Juden an und gieng mit ihm fort.
sentences.l: The peasant let that please him, put on the Jew’s coat and went off with him.
sentences.i: The peasant was content with that, put on the Jew’s coat, and went off with him.
sentences.n: “sich etwas gefallen lassen” = to put up with / accept something (here approvingly). “zog … an” = anziehen (‘to put on’).

sentences.t: Der König hielt dem Bauer die bösen Reden vor, die der Jude hinterbracht hatte.
sentences.l: The king held the wicked speeches before the peasant, which the Jew had reported.
sentences.i: The king charged the peasant with the wicked words the Jew had carried to him.
sentences.n: “jemandem etwas vorhalten” = to reproach someone with, hold something up against them. “hinterbringen” = to report secretly, carry word of.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ sprach der Bauer, „was ein Jude sagt ist immer gelogen, dem geht kein wahres Wort aus dem Munde; der Kerl da ist im Stand und behauptet ich hätte seinen Rock an.“
sentences.l: “Ah,” spoke the peasant, “what a Jew says is always a lie; not a true word comes out of his mouth; that fellow there is capable and claims I have his coat on.”
sentences.i: “Ah,” said the peasant, “whatever a Jew says is always a lie — not a true word comes from his mouth; that fellow there is quite capable of claiming I’ve got his coat on.”
sentences.n: Here the peasant cynically turns the tale’s own stereotype against the Jew to save himself. “im Stande sein” = to be capable (of). “hätte … an” = subjunctive of anhaben (‘to have on, wear’), reported claim.

sentences.t: „Was soll mir das?“ schrie der Jude, „ist der Rock nicht mein? hab ich ihn euch nicht aus bloßer Freundschaft geborgt, damit ihr vor den Herrn König treten konntet?“
sentences.l: “What is that to me?” cried the Jew, “is the coat not mine? did I not lend it to you out of mere friendship, so that you could step before the lord king?”
sentences.i: “What do you mean by that?” cried the Jew, “isn’t the coat mine? didn’t I lend it to you out of pure friendship, so you could appear before the lord king?”
sentences.n: “Was soll mir das?” = ‘what is this supposed to mean (to me)?’ “geborgt” (borgen) = lent. “konntet” = 2nd person plural past of können.

sentences.t: Wie der König das hörte, sprach er „einen hat der Jude gewiß betrogen, mich oder den Bauer,“ und ließ ihm noch etwas in harten Thalern nachzahlen.
sentences.l: As the king heard that, he spoke “one the Jew has surely cheated, me or the peasant,” and had something more paid out to him in hard thalers.
sentences.i: When the king heard that, he said, “the Jew has surely cheated one of us, either me or the peasant,” and had a further sum paid out to him in hard thalers.
sentences.n: “einen … mich oder den Bauer” = ‘one of us, me or the peasant.’ “nachzahlen” = to pay in addition. Ironically the peasant is rewarded again — the ‘good bargain’ of the title.

sentences.t: Der Bauer aber gieng in dem guten Rock und mit dem guten Geld in der Tasche heim und sprach „diesmal hab ichs getroffen.“
sentences.l: The peasant, however, went home in the good coat and with the good money in his pocket and spoke “this time I have hit it.”
sentences.i: But the peasant went home in the good coat, with the good money in his pocket, and said, “this time I’ve struck it lucky.”
sentences.n: “es treffen” = to hit it right, land luckily — the pleased close of the tale, echoing its title, ‘the good bargain.’ “hab ichs” = habe ich es.
